text: Hello, everyone. This is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California, back with an interesting video for the ETAP surgeons out there. This is a video of a robotic ETAP reef stopa repair of a recurrent incisional hernia after a supposed robotic tap.
The surgeon inserts a 5mm optical trocar through the left upper quadrant, encountering some scar tissue. Subsequently, an 8mm robotic port is successfully placed.

The surgeon uses a spinal needle to determine the trajectory for port insertion. During the process, a hole is inadvertently created in the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum, causing the space to collapse. Due to extensive scarring, the surgeon decides to abandon the attempt to create space with the laparoscope.

The surgeon attempts a five millimeter optical entry into the right retroactive space. The video shows the initial smooth entry into the areolar plane, followed by the discovery of extensive scar tissue extending caudally and laterally. Unusual structures become visible during the laparoscopic dissection.

The video shows a gap in the posterior rectus sheath, which appears very thin, with transparent tissue visible leading into the abdominal cavity. An eight millimeter robot port is inserted into the epigastrium, positioned just lateral to the left linea alba.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic crossover maneuver to access the preperitoneal space and the left retroactive space. Robot ports are placed in both retroactive spaces, and docking occurs in the upper abdomen. The hook is positioned in the right lateral, right upper quadrant port, while the scope is in the epigastric port. A twitch of the left rectus muscle is observed, indicating proximity to the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon opens the left posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying left rectus muscle. The dissection continues to locate the initial five millimeter port, which is optically pushed into the abdominal cavity to desufflate, creating more space for entry into the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand and a monopolar curved scissor in the right hand to dissect the left retroactive space, which shows significant scarring. The view is oriented caudally towards the feet, with the right rectus muscle visible on the roof and the left rectus muscle on the left side. The scarring indicates a previous TAP operation that went wrong, complicating the current ETAP procedure.

The surgeon navigates the retrorectus space using a robotic system, observing the posterior rectus sheath extending to the linea alba. Both posterior rectus sheaths are visible, along with mesh in the preperitoneal and retrorectus spaces. The surgeon dissects ventrally and dorsally to the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring no bowel is present on the preperitoneal side, and removes the rectus muscle from the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon performs a dissection in the left retrorectus space, encountering purple secure strap absorbable tackers. The rectus muscle is carefully separated from the posterior rectus sheath using cautery, with shallow scoring and downward traction to avoid bowel injury. The surgeon removes mesh from the diastatic linea alba, ensuring visibility of both dorsal and ventral sides of the posterior rectus sheath before division.

The surgeon navigates the distorted anatomy by dissecting laterally towards the linea semilunaris, searching for a clearer tissue plane amidst scar tissue. A hole is created in the peritoneum on the right side, allowing a view into the abdominal cavity to ensure no bowel is present. Cauterization is performed primarily on the muscle side to avoid damaging the posterior rectus sheath or peritoneum.

The surgeon carefully dissects the rectus muscle from the mesh, following natural tissue planes while maintaining hemostasis. The mesh is visible, and the surgeon proceeds cautiously due to the risk of encountering herniated bowel near the caudal edge of the mesh. The omentum is identified, indicating proximity to the bowel.

The surgeon encounters a challenging dissection area, mistakenly identifying muscle tissue as the posterior rectus sheath near the linea alba. The scissors touch what is later confirmed to be rectus muscle, not the true linea alba. The dissection reveals a fusion of neoperitoneum or shredded peritoneum with the rectus muscle, indicating previous surgical intervention in the retrorectus space.

The surgeon carefully dissects in a challenging area, cautiously avoiding potential bowel involvement while removing mesh from the roof of the surgical field. The hernia sac becomes visible on the right side of the screen. The surgeon identifies muscle tissue lateral to the medial edge of the rectus muscle, noting that the posterior rectus sheath is fused to the rectus muscle due to previous surgical intervention.

The surgeon bridges the gap in the posterior rectus sheath by taking down tissue, revealing purple tacks on the muscle. The dissection progresses past the defect towards the pelvis, with the caudal edge of the defect becoming visible.

The surgeon carefully removes mesh from the left rectus muscle, revealing some omentum adhered to the hernia sac. Despite initial concerns from the CT scan showing bowel in the sac, the bowel has reduced, and no adhesiolysis is necessary. The mesh appears to be closely attached to the medial edge of the anterior fascia, likely due to the previous surgeon's division of the posterior rectus sheath near the linea alba.

The surgeon continues to remove mesh and the posterior rectus sheath from the rectus muscle, exposing the anterior surface of the mesh. The omentum is carefully dissected off the ventral surface of the mesh, revealing its caudal edge. The mesh is separated from the right anterior fascia near the linea alba, with plans to sew this edge to the left side to close the posterior gap.

The surgeon continues dissection in the retrorectus space, focusing on identifying and separating the preperitoneal space from the posterior rectus sheath. The dissection involves scoring the edge of the peritonealized tissue and developing a space both ventral and dorsal to the posterior rectus sheath. The surgeon uses scissors to carefully navigate this area, ensuring the fat is brought down to isolate the posterior rectus sheath on both sides.

The surgeon divides the attenuated posterior rectus sheath in the retro-rectus space, revealing the left rectus muscle with minimal diastasis. The division continues with the more robust portion of the posterior rectus sheath above the arcuate line.

The surgeon completes the caudal dissection, ensuring good mesh overlap below the defect. The attenuated posterior rectus sheath is divided, and the retro-rectus space is maintained with the fat kept elevated. A defect is visible, and the surgeon measures it, documenting the dimensions. A large piece of mesh is placed with excellent overlap, sewn to the contralateral posterior rectus sheath to close the floor and separate the retro-rectus space from the peritoneal space.
